Facts.text: 10th recorded perihelion passage of Halley's Comet
Magister Militum of Dalamatia Julius Nepos proclaimed Emperor in Rome - rules only until 475 as the last Western Roman Emperor
Maya official Ajpach’ Waal pays an important diplomatic visit to Waxaklajuun Ub’aah K’awiil (18 Rabbit), 13th king of Copán (modern Honduras)
Vikings sail up the Loire with 67 ships and destroy Frankish-held city of Nantes
Battle of Cedynia, the first documented victory of Polish forces; Mieszko I of Poland decisively defeated Odo I of Lusatia
Afonso I of Portugal defeats army of his mother Theresa
Rindfleisch Persecutions - Jews of Ifhauben, Austria massacred
Battle of Bannockburn: Scotland regains independence from England
Jews are expelled from France for 3rd time
English fleet destroys the French at the battle of Sluys (the Hundred Years' War)
Crusaders under Earl of Nevers reach Vienna
Sultan Bajezid I releases captured crusaders for ransom
Cornish traitors Michael An Gof and Thomas Flamank are executed at Tyburn, London
7th Spanish Armada plunders the trading island of Kilwa, on the Sawhili coast of Africa, erecting a fort and establishing a garrison
Portuguese begin construction of Fort Kastela on the island of Ternate in the Maluku Islands after being encouraged to so by Sultan Bayanullah of Ternate
Gustaaf I begins Reformation in Sweden, taking Catholic possessions
Zürich and Catholic cantons sign Peace of Kappel
Anabaptist commune of Münster captured and its leaders tortured and killed
Tsar Michail's father Filaret becomes patriarch of Moscow
Mughal army begins a siege of the Portuguese trading port of Hooghly, ordered by Shah Jahan to check piracy and the slave trade (captured three months later)
Cossacks slaughter 2,000 Jews and 600 Polish Catholics in Ukraine
French fleet recaptures Duinkerk
Dutch invasion of Macau repulsed (Macau Day)
Colony of New Jersey founded when Duke of York grants Lord Berkeley and Sir George Carteret ownership of land between the Hudson and Delaware Rivers
Kingston, Jamaica, founded after Port Royal at mouth of the harbour destroyed by an earthquake
First Freemasons' Grand Lodge founded in London
Jesuit priest hold first mass in The Little Chapel in Tadoussac, New France (built 1747, now the oldest surviving church in North America)
David Rittenhouse observes a total solar eclipse in Philadelphia
First Republican constitution in France adopted
Bowdoin College is founded in Maine, USA
US and Great Britain sign the Jay Treaty, the first US extradition treaty
British under Commodore Popham and Colonel Beresford reach Buenos Aires
Battle of Beaver Dams - British and native forces defeat US forces (War of 1812)
Battle of Carabobo; Bolívar defeats royalists outside Caracas
Former slave Mary Prince is the first women to partition the UK parliament - to return to the West Indies as a free person
Fordham University (then St John's College), opens in the Bronx
Vincenzo Soliva decrees no Jew can live outside a ghetto in Italy
Residency tax on Jews of Hungary abolished
Battle of Mathias Point, Virginia Union forces attack Confederate batteries
Tennessee becomes the 11th and last state to secede from the Union
Planning an invasion of Pennsylvania, Lee's army crosses Potomac
Second Battle at Custozza: the Austrian Imperial army, joined by the Venetian Army decisively defeated the Italian army, despite the Italians' strong numerical advantage
First performance of "O Canada," the song that would become the national anthem of Canada, at the Congrès national des Canadiens-Français
200 drown as train runs off bridge near Cuautla, Mexico
National League expels umpire Richard Higham from baseball for dishonesty after his links with gambling on games are confirmed
East Cleveland Street Railway Company begins the first US commercial electric streetcar line in Cleveland, Ohio, on the Bentley-Knight system
John Lynch is the first Black person elected chairman of the Republican convention
British government of Salisbury forms
Samuel David Ferguson becomes 1st US black bishop
Marie François Sadi Carnot is assassinated in Lyon by Italian anarchist Sante Geronimo Caserio
Hail injures 26 in Topeka, Kansas
American troops drive Spanish forces from La Guasimas, Cuba
Dutch Social-Democratic Workers' party & Socialistenbond merge
Jewish National Fund starts
Russia prohibits meetings dealing with Zionism
King Peter I of Serbia names son Alexander the Prince-regent
Russian Black Sea fleet mutinies at Sebastopol
Chuvash Autonomous Region forms in RSFSR
American Professional Football Association renamed the National Football League
With declining business, the Great Gorge and International Railway begins using one-person crews on trolley operations in Canada
First detection of an airplane using reflected radio waves, a precursor to radar, by US Naval Research Laboratory engineers in Anacostia, Washington, D.C.
Ground is broken for construction of Cleveland Stadium, home of MLB Indians, 1932 to 1993, and NFL Browns, 1946-95
USSR and Afghanistan sign a neutrality treaty
Coup ends absolute monarchy in Thailand
Mary McLeod Bethune named director of Negro Affairs in National Youth Adm
500 ton meteorite lands near Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania
Pan Am's 1st US to England flight
France signs an armistice with Italy during WW II
Entire Jewish male population of Gorzhdy, Lithuania, exterminated
Germans advance into Russia and take Vilna, Brest-Litovsk and Kaunas
US Federal Communications Commission (FCC) grants 1st commercial TV licenses to New York City experimental stations W2XBS, as WNBT (NBC), and W2XAB, as WCBW (CBS)
German Africa Korps invades Egypt
US Admiral Ernest King orders Tulagi (Solomon Island) reconquered
Schermerhorn government forms in the Netherlands
The Moscow Victory Parade takes place
11.72" (29.77 cm) of rainfall at Mellen, Wisconsin (state 24-hr record)
Georges Bidault elected premier of France
Flying saucers sighted over Mount Rainier by pilot Ken Arnold
"Hopalong Cassidy" becomes 1st network western (NBC)
A. M. de Jong's murderer, Ton van Gog, arrested in Scheveningen, Netherlands
Cargo airlines are first licensed by the US Civil Aeronautics Board
French government led by prime Minister Georges Bidault resigns
New York Giants outfielder Wes Westrum hits 3 HRs & a triple in a 12-2 rout of the Cincinnati Reds at the Polo Grounds V
Persian army takes over nationalized oil installations
KSWS (now KOBR) TV channel 8 in Roswell, NM (NBC) begins broadcasting
WISC TV channel 3 in Madison, WI (CBS) begins broadcasting
The U.S. Supreme Court rules that obscenity is not protected by the First Amendment in Roth v. United States
Geoff Griffin takes a hat-trick South Africa v England Lord's
Outfielder Jack Reed hits his only MLB home run in the 22nd-inning as NY Yankees edge Detroit Tigers, 9-7 in slowest extra-inning game in league history; 7:00 hours
First demonstration of a home video recorder at BBC Studios in London
Levi Eshkol forms 11th Israeli government
Zanzibar granted internal self-government by Britain
FTC rules health warnings must appear on all cigarette packages
In South Africa, the 90-Days Act commences, providing for any person to be detained, without trial, for 90 days; further, the person could be re-arrested under the same law for another 90 days
Period of relative peace following WWII exceeds that following WWI
Zaire adopts constitution
Australia all out for 78 v England at Lord's
Deadline for redeeming silver certificate dollars for silver bullion
Detroit outfielder Jim Northrup hits 2 grand-slams to help the Tigers beat the Cleveland Indians, 14-3
Resurrection City in Washington, D.C. closed permanently
"Catch 22" opens in movie theaters
Reds play final game at Cincinnati's Crosley Field, beat Giants 5-4
US Senate votes overwhelmingly to repeal Gulf of Tonkin Resolution
Single "Troglodyte (Cave Man)" by Jimmy Castor Bunch peaks at #6
South African runner Danie Malan sets a new 1,000m world record 2:16.0 in Munich, Germany
The Irish Republican Army (IRA) kill 3 British Army soldiers in a land mine attack near Dungiven, County Derry
Wake Island becomes unincorporated territory of US (US Air Force)
Yvonne Brathwaite Burke becomes 1st black chair of a Democratic convention
Marlene Raymond (15), limboes under a flaming bar at 6 1/8"
India is dismissed for 42 in 17 overs in 2nd Test defeat to England at Lord's; lowest total in country's Test cricket history; Chris Old 5-21, Geoff Arnold 4-19
Kansas City pitcher Steve Busby retires first 9 White Sox to set AL record with 33 consecutive batsmen retired; Royals still lose, 3-1 to Chicago
Eastern 727 crashes at JFK Airport NY, kills 113
Moon tremor perceived (hit by Taurid meteors)
1975 movie "The Rocky Horror Picture Show" released in Germany

A Principal's office in Hlengisi Primary, Nyanga, outside Cape Town, South Africa, is burnt down; part of the broader resistance against the oppressive new Bantu Education policy of Afrikaans as a teaching medium in their schools
Josiah Mutabuzi Isaya Kibira is the first African to be elected president of the Lutheran World Federation (LWF)
Affirmed wins $500,000 Hollywood Cup, 1st horse to win $2 million
1st French: spationaute Jean-Loup Chrétien and 2 Soviet cosmonauts, lift off aboard Soyuz T-6 from the Baikonur Cosmodrome in Kazakhstan, USSR
US Supreme Court rules president can't be sued for actions in office
7th NASA Space Shuttle Mission: Challenger 2 lands at Edwards AFB
18th NASA Space Shuttle Mission (51-G): Discovery 5 returns to Earth
Challenger moves to Vehicle Assembly Building at Kennedy Space Center (Florida) for mating of STS 51-F
Guy Hunt elected 1st Republican governor of Alabama in 112 years
US Senate approves "tax reform"
CFL's Montreal Alouettes team folds
Minor League Salt Lake City Trappers begin pro baseball record 29 consecutive win streak
Cleveland pitcher Doug Jones sets record of 14 consecutive saves
Red Sox begin AL record 23rd consecutive home win streak
CardsVince Colemansteals record 39th & 40th consecutive bases
NHL adopts instant-replay and tenth of second clock in final minute
Eddie Antar, American CEO (Crazy Eddies), facing $74M stock fraud charges, caught in Israel
Arab terrorist group planning bombing of Holland and Lincoln Tunnels caught
Yale computer science professor Dr. David Gelernter loses the sight in one eye, the hearing in one ear, and part of his right hand after receiving a mailbomb from the Unabomber
1st French "all news" TV (LCI) begins broadcasting
Stanley Cup Final, Meadowlands Arena, East Rutherford, NJ: New Jersey Devils beat Detroit Red Wings, 5-2 for a 4-0 series sweep; Devils' first Stanley Cup finals appearance
Wrapped Reichstag art installation completed by artists Christo and Jeanne Claude; German parliament building completely wrapped by polypropylene fabric
Melissa Drexler, 18, charged with killing her baby during her prom
USAF reports Roswell 'space aliens' were dummies
NBA Draft: Pacific center Michael Olowokandi first pick by Los Angeles Clippers
The Igandu train disaster in Tanzania kills 281, the worst train accident in African history
Capital punishment is declared unconstitutional in New York

Christie’s Auction House in New York City conducts sale of 80 ofEric Claptonand friends' guitars to benefit Crossroads Centre at Antigua, a drug and alcohol residential rehabilitation facility he co-founded in 1997, raising over $5M; "Blackie", a 1956-57 composite Fender Stratocaster used as his primary instrument form the mid 70s until 1985 sells for a then record $959,500
NBA Draft: Southwest Atlanta Christian Academy power forward Dwight Howard first pick by Orlando Magic
The Angora Fire starts near South Lake Tahoe, California destroying 200+ structures in its first 48 hours
Eton College founded in England byHenry VI
Byzantine princess Sophia Palaiologina departs Rome with a large entourage for Moscow to marryIvan III
James IVis crowned King of Scotland at Scone Abbey in Scone, aged 15
John Cabotclaims parts of North America for England in what is now Eastern Canada, believing he has found Asia in Newfoundland
Henry VIIIis crowned King of England in Westminster Abbey, London
Paracelsuspublicly burns standard medical textbooks at the University of Basel, as a protest against the current teaching and practice of medicine
English KingHenry VIIIcommands his 4th wife,Anne of Cleves, to leave the court
Five clergymen of Dutch city Enkhuizen are hanged during the Dutch Rebellion againstPhilip II of Spain
Micmac chiefMembertouand 20 of his family are the first people of the First Nations to be baptized by Christian missionaries in Port-Royal, New France
Freemason and Mayor of PhiladelphiaWilliam Allenis appointed Provincial Grand Master of Pennsylvania, the first and youngest Grand Master of the Grand Lodge of Pennsylvania
The Kingswood School is opened byJohn Wesleyand his brother Charles Wesley in Bristol, England; the school later moves to Bath
Napoleon Bonaparte's Grand Armée numbering half a million begin their invasion of Russia by crossing the Nieman River
US PresidentFranklin Piercesigns the Gadsden Purchase, buying 29,670 square-miles (76,800 square km) from Mexico for $10 million (now southern Arizona and New Mexico)
Battle of Solferino, Northern Italy: French underNapoleon IIIand a Sardinian army underVictor Emmanuel IIdefeat the Austrian army led by EmperorFranz Joseph I
First exhibition byPablo Picasso, aged 19, opens in Paris
British KingEdward VIIdevelops appendicitis, delaying his coronation
Target Corporation is founded by American businessmanGeorge Daytonas Goodfellow Dry Goods in Minneapolis
French couturierPaul Poiretholds his infamous 'The 1002nd Night' costume ball to launch his “Parfums de Rosine", 1st signature scent linked to a design house
Mary Pickfordbecomes the first female film star to receive a million-dollar contract
Adolf Hitlerbegins a month long prison sentence for paramilitary operations; he rails against the 'Jewish sell-out' of Germany to the Bolsheviks
Charter NFL club Chicago Staleys renamed Chicago Bears by team founder, owner and head coachGeorge Halas
PopePius XIspeaks against allies occupying Ruhrgebied
Rookie outfielderJoe DiMaggioties 3 MLB records in New York's 10-run 5th inning against the White Sox, hitting 2 home runs for 8 total bases; Yankees beat Chicago, 18-4

Village of Ležáky, Czechoslovakia destroyed by Nazis after Gestapo finds a radio transmitter believed to have been involved coordinating the assassination ofReinhard Heydrich, 33 adults were executed by firing squad on site, and children were sent to concentration camp gas chambers, and the village was burned down and plowed under
Future Baseball Hall of Fame infielderJackie Robinsonsteals home for first of 19 times in his career as the Brooklyn Dodgers beat Pittsburgh Pirates, 4-2
Republican National Convention in Philadelphia nominates Governor of NYThomas E. Dewey
Harmon Killebrewhits his 1st HR (off Billy Hoeff)
"TheSteve AllenShow" returns on NBC-TV
Patrice Lumumbaas head of the MNC party forms a government in the Congo and becomes independent Congo's first Prime Minister
Tony Sheridan, backed by the Beatles, records "If You Love Me Baby" (aka "Take Out Some Insurance On Me, Baby") in Hamburg, Germany
PopePaul VIpublishes encyclical Sacerdotalis coelibatus
Joe Frazierstops Mexican challenger Manuel Ramos in 2nd round TKO at NYC's Madison Square Garden in his first heavyweight boxing title defense
Bobby Murcerties record of 4 consecutive HRs
Warner Bros. releases "McCabe & Mrs. Miller", a western film byRobert Altman, starringWarren BeattyandJulie Christie, with songs byLeonard Cohen
Crystal Gaylereleases best-selling album "We Must Believe in Magic" featuring worldwide hit "Don't It Make My Brown Eyes Blue" (1st platinum album by a female country music artist)
IRS reveals US PresidentJimmy Carterpaid no taxes in 1976
Rickey Hendersonmakes MLB debut with for Oakland A's and steals his 1st base
"For Your Eyes Only", 12th James Bond, starringRoger Mooreand 1st drected by John Glen premieres in London
Equal Rights Amendment goes down to defeat
Don Suttonbecomes 8th pitcher to strikeout 3,000 batters
MLB Cincinnati RedsJoe Morgansets career HR mark for 2nd basemen with #265
In Britain, Foreign Office Minister Lynda Chalker, meetsOliver Tambo, president of the African National Congress to discuss means of ending Apartheid without violence
Jiang Zeminappointed the General Secretary of the Central Committee of the Communist Party of China
Billy Joel(43) gets diploma from Hicksville (New York) High School, 25 years after leaving with a missing English credit; he submitted samples of his writing to earn his degree
Mafia bossJohn Gottibegins life sentence in jail for murder and other crimes
MLB CommissionerFay Vincentpermanently bans pitcher Steve Howe from baseball for repeated substance abuse violations; later overturned on appeal
NBA Draft: LSU centerShaquille O'Nealfirst pick by Orlando Magic
3rd Rugby World Cup, Ellis Park, Johannesburg: Springboks fly-half Joel Stransky lands the winning drop goal in extra time as South Africa defeats New Zealand 15-12
Despite an AL record 19-strikeout performance by Randy Johnson, A's first basemanMark McGwirehits a massive 538 foot home run in Oakland's 4-1 defeat of Seattle Mariners

Christie’s Auction House in New York City conducts sale of 100 ofEric Clapton’s guitars to benefit Crossroads Centre at Antigua, a drug and alcohol residential rehabilitation facility he co-founded in 1997, raising nearly $4.5M; "Brownie", a 1956 Fender Stratocaster used on the original recording of "Layla" sells for a then record $450,000
"Pirates of the Caribbean: Dead Man's Chest", directed by Gore Verbinski, starringJohnny Depp,Keira KnightleyandOrlando Bloom, premieres at Disneyland - becomes fastest film to gross over 1 billion
Michael Bay's film "Transformers: Revenge of the Fallen" is released, grossing over $832 million worldwide
Mike Babcockis named head coach of Team Canada for the 2010 Winter Olympics (they would win the gold medal)
"TheTwilight Saga: Eclipse", based on the book byStephenie Meyer, directed by David Slade, starringKristen StewartandRobert Pattinson, premieres in Los Angeles
Sudden outbreak of St. John's Dance causes people in the streets of Aachen, Germany, to experience hallucinations and begin to jump and twitch uncontrollably until they collapse from exhaustion
Soviet Union blockades West Berlin by stopping access by road, rail and water
Henry I, the Child, 1st count of Hessen (1256-1308), born in Leuven, Flemish Brabant, Flanders (d. 1308)
John de Warenne, 7th Earl of Surrey, English politician, supporter of Edward II and the last Warenne earl of Surrey, born in Conisborough, England (d. 1347)
Johanna, Duchess of Brabant, ruling duchess of Brabant from 1355 to her death; however, her brother-in-law Louis II of Flanders occupied the Duchy from 1356, born in Brussels (d. 1406)
Jeanne de Valois, Queen of Navarre, born in Châteauneuf-sur-Loire, France (d. 1373)
Saint John of Capistrano, Franciscan friar and Catholic priest from the Italian town of Capestrano, Abruzzo, born in Capestrano, Abruzzi, Kingdom of Naples (d. 1456)
Johannes Bugenhagen, German reformer, born in Wollin, Pomerania, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1558)
Johannes Brenz, German theologist (Dutch Brentians), born in Weil der Stadt, Stuttgart (d. 1570)
Theodore Beza, French Reformed Protestant theologian, born in Vezelay, Burgundy, Kingdom of France (d. 1605)
Robert Dudley, English Earl of Leicester, favourite of Elizabeth I, English commander-in-chief in the Netherlands, born in England (d. 1588)
Joanna of Austria, Princess of Portugal, born in Madrid (d. 1573)
St. John of the Cross [Juan de Yepes y Álvarez], Spanish Carmelite mystic, saint, priest and poet, born in Fontiveros, Ávila, Spain (d. 1591)
Robert Parsons, English jesuit (A Letter Disclosure), born in Nether Stowey, Somerset, England (d. 1610)
Rombout Hogerbeets, Dutch lawyer and City Solicitor of Leiden, born in Hoorn, Netherlands (d. 1625)

Samuel Ampzing, Dutch minister, poet, translator (Bijbel-poezije - Bible Poetry), and linguist (Taelbericht der Nederlandsche spellinge - Treatise on Dutch Selling), born in Haarlem, Netherlands (d. 1632)
Rijcklof Volckertsz van Goens, Governor-General of the Dutch East Indies (1678-81), born in Rees, Germany (d. 1682)
Jean Baptiste Massillon, French churchman, born in Hyères, France (d. 1742)
François van Aerssen, Dutch vice-admiral (Suriname, Algeria), born in Paris (d. 1740)
Johann Albrecht Bengel, German scholar of Greek (Greek New Testament), born in Winnenden, Württemberg (d. 1757)
Jean-Jacques Burlamaqui, Genevan legal and political theorist, born in Geneva (d. 1748)
Jean-Baptiste de Boyer, Marquis d'Argens, French writer, born in Aix-en-Provence, France (d. 1771)
Jean-Baptiste Rochefort, French composer, born in Paris (d. 1819)
Johann Melchior Dreyer, German composer, born in Röttingen, Duchy of Württemberg, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1824)
Johann Paul Wessely [Jan Pavel Veselý], Czech violinist and composer, born in Hluboká nad Vltavou (Frauenberg), Kingdom of Bohemia (d. 1810)
Éleuthère Irénée du Pont, French-American chemist and industrialist (E. I. du Pont de Nemours and Company), born in Paris (d. 1834)
François-Nicolas-Benoît Haxo, French Army general, born in Lunéville, France (d. 1838)
John Ross, Scottish naval officer and Artic explorer (rediscovered Baffin Bay), born in Balsarroch, Scotland (d. 1856)
Thomas Blanchard, American machinist and inventor, born in Sutton, Massachusetts (d. 1864)
Ernst Heinrich Weber, German anatomist and physiologist (one of the founders of experimental psychology), born in Wittenberg, Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1878)
John Hughes, 1st Archbishop of the Archdiocese of New York, born in Annaloghan, County Tyrone, Kingdom of Ireland (d. 1864)
George James Webb, English-American composer, born in Salisbury, Wiltshire, England (d. 1887)
Stephan Endlicher, Austrian botanist, born in Pressburg (Pozsony), Kingdom of Hungary (d. 1849)
Willard Richards, American physician and religious leader, born in Hopkinton, Massachusetts (d. 1854)
Anna Caroline Oury (née De Belleville), German concert pianist and composer, born in Landshut, Kingdom of Bavaria (d. 1880)
John Archibald Campbell, American jurist and Supreme Court justice (1853–61), born in Washington, Georgia (d. 1889)
Henry Ward Beecher, American clergyman and orator (Independent), born in Litchfield, Connecticut (d. 1887)
Henry R. Jackson, American Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Athens, Georgia (d. 1898)
Birkett D. Fry, American Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Kanawha County, Virginia (d. 1891)

Grand Duchess Alexandra Nikolaevna of Russia, youngest daughter and fourth child of Tsar Nicholas I, Emperor of Russia, and his wife, Princess Charlotte of Prussia, born in St. Petersburg, Russian Empire (d. 1844)
George Goyder, English surveyor-general of South Australia, born in Liverpool, England (d. 1898)
Edward Harland, American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Norwich, Connecticut (d. 1915)
Gustav von Schmoller, German economist (Lectern Socialism), born in Heilbronn, Germany (d. 1917)
Gustavus Franklin Swift, American founder of the meatpacking firm Swift & Company, born in West Sandwich, Massachusetts (d. 1903)
Louis Brassin, Belgian composer and pianist, born in Aix-la-Chapelle (d. 1884)
Gaston Salvayre, French composer, born in Toulouse, France (d. 1916)
Brooks Adams, American philosopher (Theory of Social Revolutions), born in Quincy, Massachusetts (d. 1927)
Victor Adler, Austrian politician (Minister of Foreign Affairs), born in Prague, Bohemia, Austrian Empire (d. 1918)
Mercedes of Orléans, Queen of Spain, born in Palacio Real, Madrid, Spain (d. 1878)
Robert Henri, American painter (The Eight) and leader of Ashcan school of painting, born in Cincinnati, Ohio (d. 1929)
Horatio Mbelle, South African interpreter, community leader and politician, born in Burgersdorp, Cape Colony (d. 1947)
Thomas Carl Whitmer, American composer, born in Altoona, Pennsylvania (d. 1959)
Aleksei M. Remizov, Russian writer (Iveren), born in Moscow (d. 1957)
Agrippina J. Vaganova, Russian ballet dancer, and pedagogue (Fundamentals of the Classical Dance), born in Saint Petersburg, Russian Empire (d. 1951)
Athanase David, Canadian politician and businessman, born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 1953)
Carl Diem, German sports executive (Sec Gen Organising Committee 1936 Berlin Olympics; creator Olympic torch relay), born in Würzburg, Germany (d. 1962)
George Shiels, Irish dramatist, known for "The Rugged Path", born in Ballymoney, County Antrim (d. 1949)
Gerrit Rietveld, Dutch architect and furniture designer (Juliana Hall, Sonsbeek Pavilion), born in Utrecht, Netherlands (d. 1964)
Irving Pichel, American actor and director ("Madame Butterfly"; "Oliver Twist"), born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 1954)
Roy O. Disney, American businessman and co-founder of the Walt Disney Company with brother Walt, born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1971)
Daniel K. Ludwig, American shipping magnate, founded Exportadora de Sal, born in South Haven Michigan (d. 1992)
Bruce Marshall, Scottish writer (Father Malachy's Miracle), born in Edinburgh, Scotland (d. 1987)
Wilhelm Cauer, German mathematician who pioneered network synthesis, born in Berlin, Germany (d. 1945)
Chuck Taylor, American Basketball Hall of Fame promoter (Converse All Star shoes), born in Azalia, Indiana (d. 1969)
Harry Partch, American composer (Revelations in the Courthouse Park; Delusion Of The Fury), and instrument inventor, born in Oakland, California (d. 1974)[1]
Marcel Mule, French classical saxophonist, and founder of the French Saxophone School, born in Aube, France (d. 2001)

(Wonga) Phil Harris, American singer, actor (The Jungle Book; The Aristocrats; Robin Hood), comedian, and orchestra leader (The Jack Benny Program; The Phil Harris-Alice Faye Show), born in Linton, Indiana (d. 1995)
Kurt Kusenberg, German writer (Wein Auf Lebenszeit), born in Gothenburg, Sweden (d. 1983)
Pierre Fournier, French cellist, and teacher (Paris Conservatoire, 1937-49), born in Paris, France (d. 1986)
Willard Maas, American educator and experimental filmmaker, born in Lindsay, California (d. 1971)
Arseny Tarkovsky, Russian poet and translator, born in Yelisavetgrad, Russia (d. 1989)
José de Lima Siqueira, Brazilian conductor, and composer, born in Conceição, State of Paraíba, Brazil (d. 1985)
Rollie Hemsley, American baseball catcher (5-time MLB All Star), born in Syracuse, Ohio (d. 1972)
Alfons Rebane, Estonian military officer during WWII and MI6 intelligence officer, born in Valga, Livonia, Russian Empire (d. 1976)
Guru Gopinath, Indian classical dancer, born in Champakulam, Kuttanad (now Kerala), British India (d. 1987)
Hugo Distler, German organist, choral conductor and composer, born in Nuremberg, Kingdom of Bavaria (now Germany) (d. 1942)
Jean Deslauriers, Canadian violin prodigy, conductor (CBC, 1935-78), and composer, born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 1978)
Milton Katims, American violist and conductor (Seattle Symphony, 1954–76), born in New York City (d. 2006)
William Penney, British physicist (developed Britain's 1st atom bomb), born in Gibraltar (d. 1991)
Denis Dowling, New Zealand-born British operatic baritone, born in Ranfurly, South Island (d. 1996)
Ernesto Sábato, Argentinian writer (Sábato Report), born in Rojas, Argentina (d. 2011)
Portia White, Canadian contralto, and educator, born in Truro, Nova Scotia (d. 1968)
Brian Johnston, British cricket commentator (BBC 1946-94) and cake connoisseur, born in Little Berkhamsted, England (d. 1994)
Norman Cousins, American editor (Saturday Review) and peace activist, born in West Hoboken, New Jersey (d. 1990)
Selwyn Powell, British magazine editor (The Geographical Magazine; Night & Day), born in England (d. 1994)
Jan Kubiš, Czech paratrooper in WWII (killed Nazi head of Bohemia Reinhard Heydrich in Operation Anthropoid), born in Dolní Vilémovice, Moravia, Austria-Hungary (d. 1942)
Max van Praag, Dutch popular music singer (Glück Auf!), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1991)
Pearl Witherington, British WW II secret agent for the Special Operations Executive, born in Paris, France (d. 2008)
Charles Dunn, British scholar and professor of Japanese studies, born in United Kingdom (d. 1995)
Fred Hoyle, English astronomer (proposed steady-state universe theory, coined phrase 'Big Bang'), born in Gilstead, England (d. 2001)
John Ciardi, American poet, translator (Dante's "Divine Comedy"), and literary commentator, born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1986)
Ruth Shaw Wylie, American composer (Spring Madness; Psychogram; Nova), and educator (Wayne State University, 1949-69), born in Cincinnati, Ohio (d. 1989)
Joan Clarke, English cryptanalyst and code-breaker at Bletchley Park during World War II (Enigma Project), born in London (d. 1996)[1]
Ramblin' Tommy Scott, American country music and rockabilly guitarist, born near Toccoa, Georgia (d. 2013)
Albert "Al" Molinaro, American actor (The Odd Couple, Happy Days), born in Kenosha, Wisconsin (d. 2015)
Don Salls, American football linebacker (Alabama) and coach (Jacksonville State University 1946-52, 1954-64), born in Trenton, New Jersey (d. 2021)
Michael Schofield, British sociologist and writer (Society and the Homosexual, The Strange Case of Pot), born in Leeds, England (d. 2014)
Bernhard Krol, German hornist and composer (Figaro Metamorphoses), born in Berlin, Gemany (d. 2013)[1]
Jack Carter [Chakrin], American comedian and actor, born in Brooklyn New York (d. 2015)
Roy Elihu Travis, American composer (Passion of Oedipus), born in New York City (d. 2013)
Tata Giacobetti, Italian jazz singer (Quartetto Cetra), born in Rome, Italy (d. 1988)
Margaret Olley, Australian still-life and interiors painter, born in Lismore, Australia (d. 2011)
Brian Bevan, Australian Rugby League HOF winger (Warrington 620 games, 740 tries), born in Sydney, Australia (d. 1991)
Kurt Furgler, Swiss politician, President of the Confederation (1977, 1981 and 1985), born in St Gallen, Switzerland d. 2008)
Ranasinghe Premadasa, President of Sri Lanka (1989-93), born in Colombo, British Ceylon (d. 1993)
Martin Lewis Perl, American physicist (1995 Nobel Prize for Physics for discovery of the tau lepton particle), born in New York City (d. 2014)
Herb Klein, American businessman, lawyer, and politician (US Representative from New Jersey, 1993-95), born in Newark, New Jersey (d. 2023)
William B. Ziff Jr., American magazine publishing executive, (Modern Bride, Popular Electronics, PC Magazine), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2006)
